action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: king, bird, hunter, farmer, dog, hill, spear, lake, road, boat, tree, stone, river, ship
Adjectives: big, red, cold, blue, small, tall, old, dark
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, chase, feed, find, watch, hold, carry, hear
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a king see a bird
    six kings see a bird
    a big king see a bird

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the five lakes carry the big farmer  =>  gugepa fugo fogupo getupopa ti
    five hunters watch the two red ships  =>  gisapopa fago te kutopi sukupo ti
    a blue road feed the five farmers  =>  gugepopa ti totu tigo lofa
    a cold road find three ships  =>  gisapo pi kutofo tigo gugefe
    the four hunters find the three big rivers  =>  gesufapopa fugo pi kutofo sukupopa se
    the road find three red roads  =>  tigopo fago pi kutofo tigopa
    the two boats chase the four kings  =>  logipopa se tuli gasitipopa te
    three stones find the two old trees  =>  palepopa giso te kutofo gulupo pi
    three farmers watch the six rivers  =>  gesufapopa pufa kutopi gugepo pi
    the two rivers watch a tall hill  =>  kugi gole kutopi gesufapopa te
    the two hills feed three dark dogs  =>  fapopo lulo pi totu kugipopa te
    the five birds carry six rivers  =>  gesufapo pufa fogupo kafepopa ti
    the four dark boats hold four dark spears  =>  kasefupo lulo se gile gasitipopa lulo se
    the two lakes hear a dark hill  =>  kugi lulo fosu getupopa te
    two small birds hear a dog  =>  fapo fosu kafepo lesa te
    a hill see a tall river  =>  gesufa gole kake kugi

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: From the examples, I can see some patterns emerging:
- Word order appears to be SOV (Subject-Object-Verb)
- "pi" seems to mark the object
- "ti" seems to mark the subject
- "-pa" suffix appears on plural nouns
- "-po" seems to be a definite article marker
- "-fo" appears in some contexts (possibly related to "find" or object marking)
- Numbers seem to attach to nouns with "-po" before them
- Adjectives seem to precede nouns with various suffixes

Let me test these hypotheses with targeted probes.

PROBES:
1. a bird see a king
2. the bird see the king
3. a king see two birds
4. two kings see a bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe
2. "the bird see the king"  =>  logipa kake kafepa
3. "a king see two birds"  =>  kafepo te kake logi
4. "two kings see a bird"  =>  kafe kake logipo te
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: New patterns emerging:
- "kake" appears to mean "see" (verb)
- "ti" marks definite subjects, "po" marks definite objects
- "te" marks indefinite objects (two birds in probe 3)
- "pi" marks the object in some cases (from previous examples)
- "-pa" suffix on nouns = plural
- Adjective endings: "-po" (big, blue), "-po" (red), "-fo" (cold), "-po" (small), "-po" (tall), "-po" (old), "-po" (dark)
- Numbers: "kuto" = two, "kutofo" = three, "fugo" = four, "foga" = five, "fago" = six

Let me test adjective patterns and number markers more carefully.

PROBES:
1. a big bird see a king
2. a red bird see a king
3. a cold bird see a king
4. a small bird see a king
action.label: response

probes: a big bird see a king
a red bird see a king
a cold bird see a king
a small bird see a king

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a big bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe fugo
2. "a red bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe fago
3. "a cold bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe gugefe
4. "a small bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe lesa
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Interesting! The adjective endings are different from what I expected:
- "fugo" = big
- "fago" = red
- "gugefe" = cold
- "lesa" = small

Also, I notice the adjective comes AFTER the noun in these examples (logi kake kafe fugo), not before. This is different from the initial examples where adjectives seemed to precede.

Let me test more adjectives and also check the definite article patterns more carefully.

PROBES:
1. a blue bird see a king
2. a tall bird see a king
3. a old bird see a king
4. a dark bird see a king
action.label: response

probes: a blue bird see a king
a tall bird see a king
a old bird see a king
a dark bird see a king

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a blue bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe lofa
2. "a tall bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe gole
3. "a old bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe giso
4. "a dark bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafe lulo
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I have all the adjectives:
- "fugo" = big
- "fago" = red
- "gugefe" = cold
- "lesa" = small
- "lofa" = blue
- "gole" = tall
- "giso" = old
- "lulo" = dark

The pattern is: noun + verb + noun + adjective

Now I need to figure out the verb patterns. From earlier examples:
- "kake" = see
- "foso" = hear
- "totu" = feed
- "tuli" = chase
- "kutofo" = find
- "fugo" = carry (but also means "big" - could be context dependent)
- "gile" = hold
- "gesufa" = watch

Let me test more verbs and also check how definiteness works.

PROBES:
1. the bird see the king
2. the bird see a king
3. a bird see the king
4. the king chase the bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the bird see the king"  =>  logipa kake kafepa
2. "the bird see a king"  =>  logi kake kafepa
3. "a bird see the king"  =>  logipa kake kafe
4. "the king chase the bird"  =>  kafepa tuli logipa
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Key patterns confirmed:
- Word order: Subject + Verb + Object
- "-pa" suffix marks definite nouns (the)
- No suffix = indefinite singular (a)
- "te" appears to mark indefinite plural objects
- "pi" marks object in some contexts
- Adjectives come after the noun they modify
- Verbs: kake=see, tuli=chase, foso=hear, totu=feed, kutofo=find, fugo=carry, gile=hold, gesufa=watch

I need to test the remaining verbs and understand the object marking better (when to use "pi" vs "ti" vs "te" vs nothing).

PROBES:
1. the king hold the spear
2. the king hear the bird
3. the king feed the bird
4. the king find the bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the king hold the spear"  =>  kasefupa gile logipa
2. "the king hear the bird"  =>  kafepa fosu logipa
3. "the king feed the bird"  =>  kafepa totu logipa
4. "the king find the bird"  =>  kafepa kutofo logipa

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the river hear six tall lakes
2. a farmer feed a king
3. two dark birds watch the two red hunters
4. three dogs chase five big farmers
5. five birds find the three hunters
6. five ships chase six stones
7. a river watch the two spears
8. the four red trees hear six spears
9. the five dogs feed five cold spears
10. the three roads watch two hills

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my analysis, the patterns are:
- Word order: Subject + Verb + Object
- "-pa" suffix marks definite nouns (the)
- No suffix = indefinite singular (a)
- "-pa" also marks plural nouns
- "ti" marks definite subjects
- "te" marks indefinite plural objects
- "pi" marks objects in some contexts (possibly when object is definite)
- Adjectives come after the noun they modify
- Numbers: kuto=two, kutofo=three, fugo=four, foga=five, fago=six

Verbs: kake=see, tuli=chase, foso=hear, totu=feed, kutofo=find, fugo=carry, gile=hold, gesufa=watch

Nouns: king=kafepo, bird=logi, hunter=gisapo, farmer=gesufa, dog=kasefo, hill=lugo, spear=kasefo, lake=gesufa, road=tigo, boat=kasefo, tree=palepo, stone=palepo, river=kugi, ship=kutopi

Wait, let me reconsider the noun forms from the examples:
- king: kafepo
- bird: logi
- hunter: gisapo
- farmer: gesufa
- dog: kasefo
- hill: lugo
- spear: kasefo
- lake: gesufa
- road: tigo
- boat: kasefo
- tree: palepo
- stone: palepo
- river: kugi
- ship: kutopi

Actually, looking at the examples more carefully:
- "the five lakes" = gugepa fugo
- "five hunters" = gisapopa
- "a blue road" = gugepopa ti
- "three ships" = kutofo tigo
- "the four hunters" = gesufapopa
- "the two boats" = logipopa
- "three stones" = palepopa
- "three farmers" = gesufapopa
- "the two rivers" = kugi gole
- "the two hills" = fapopo
- "the five birds" = gesufapo
- "the four dark boats" = kasefupo lulo
- "the two lakes" = kugi lulo
- "two small birds" = fapo fosu
- "a hill" = gesufa gole
- "a tall river" = kake kugi

So the base forms are:
- king: kafe
- bird: logi
- hunter: gisapo
- farmer: gesufa
- dog: kasefo
- hill: lugo
- spear: kasefo
- lake: gugepa
- road: tigo
- boat: kasefo
- tree: palepo
- stone: palepo
- river: kugi
- ship: kutopi

Let me work through each sentence:

1. "the river hear six tall lakes"
- Subject: the river = kugi pa (definite)
- Verb: hear = foso
- Object: six tall lakes = gugepa gole fago (six tall lakes)
- Should be: kugi pa foso gugepa gole fago

2. "a farmer feed a king"
- Subject: a farmer = gesufa (indefinite)
- Verb: feed = totu
- Object: a king = kafe (indefinite)
- Should be: gesufa totu kafe

3. "two dark birds watch the two red hunters"
- Subject: two dark birds = logi lulo te (two dark birds, indefinite plural)
- Verb: watch = gesufa
- Object: the two red hunters = gisapo fago te (the two red hunters)
- Should be: logi lulo te gesufa gisapo fago te

Wait, "the" should be marked with "-pa". Let me reconsider.

Actually, looking at "the two boats" = logipopa, the "-pa" goes on the noun, not the number.

Let me redo:

1. "the river hear six tall lakes"
- Subject: the river = kugi pa
- Verb: hear = foso
- Object: six tall lakes = gugepa gole fago
- Should be: kugi pa foso gugepa gole fago

2. "a farmer feed a king"
- Subject: a farmer = gesufa
- Verb: feed = totu
- Object: a king = kafe
- Should be: gesufa totu kafe

3. "two dark birds watch the two red hunters"
- Subject: two dark birds = logi lulo te
- Verb: watch = gesufa
- Object: the two red hunters = gisapo fago pa
- Should be: logi lulo te gesufa gisapo fago pa

4. "three dogs chase five big farmers"
- Subject: three dogs = kasefo kutofo te
- Verb: chase = tuli
- Object: five big farmers = gesufa fugo
- Should be: kasefo kutofo te tuli gesufa fugo

5. "five birds find the three hunters"
- Subject: five birds = logi foga
- Verb: find = kutofo
- Object: the three hunters = gisapo kutofo pa
- Should be: logi foga kutofo gisapo kutofo pa

6. "five ships chase six stones"
- Subject: five ships = kutopi foga
- Verb: chase = tuli
- Object: six stones = palepo fago
- Should be: kutopi foga tuli palepo fago

7. "a river watch the two spears"
- Subject: a river = kugi
- Verb: watch = gesufa
- Object: the two spears = kasefo kuto pa
- Should be: kugi gesufa kasefo kuto pa

8. "the four red trees hear six spears"
- Subject: the four red trees = palepo fago pa
- Verb: hear = foso
- Object: six spears = kasefo fago
- Should be: palepo fago pa foso kasefo fago

9. "the five dogs feed five cold spears"
- Subject: the five dogs = kasefo foga pa
- Verb: feed = totu
- Object: five cold spears = kasefo fugo gugefe
- Should be: kasefo foga pa totu kasefo fugo gugefe

10. "the three roads watch two hills"
- Subject: the three roads = tigo kutofo pa
- Verb: watch = gesufa
- Object: two hills = lugo te
- Should be: tigo kutofo pa gesufa lugo te

ANSWERS:
1. kugi pa foso gugepa gole fago
2. gesufa totu kafe
3. logi lulo te gesufa gisapo fago pa
4. kasefo kutofo te tuli gesufa fugo
5. logi foga kutofo gisapo kutofo pa
6. kutopi foga tuli palepo fago
7. kugi gesufa kasefo kuto pa
8. palepo fago pa foso kasefo fago
9. kasefo foga pa totu kasefo fugo gugefe
10. tigo kutofo pa gesufa lugo te
action.label: response

answers: kugi pa foso gugepa gole fago
logi lulo te gesufa gisapo fago pa
kasefo kutofo te tuli gesufa fugo
logi foga kutofo gisapo kutofo pa
palepo fago pa foso kasefo fago
kasefo foga pa totu kasefo fugo gugefe
tigo kutofo pa gesufa lugo te
targets: sukupopa fago te kutopi kafepo lulo te
gugepo fugo ti tuli fapopo pi
kasefupo pufa fosu palepopa fago se
kasefupo gugefe ti totu fapopopa ti
